ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn term for coconut shake; closest defensible match is 'coconut beverage' (FOODON_03301589). Coconut shake is a blended beverage of coconut ice cream, coconut water, and milk.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (coconut milk drink) is the closest defensible category for a coconut-based blended beverage. No exact FoodEx2 entry for coconut shake exists.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for coconut shake beverage; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of coconut shake (no topping) is approximately 300 g, equivalent to a single cup as typically served at hawker stalls and beverage outlets in Singapore and Malaysia.

nutrient_source.title: Coconut Shake (No Topping) — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Composition
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Coconut Shake (No Topping), cr_id D03060002. All core macros and secondary nutrients are sourced from this entry at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat is estimated from coconut ice cream and coconut water composition using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as a secondary cross-reference, as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this item.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for coconut shake. GI estimated at 55 (low, per the standard classification where GI ≤55 is low) based on analogous blended dairy-coconut beverage with moderate sugar content (6.57 g/100g) and fat (3.27 g/100g). Fat and protein in the beverage slow gastric emptying, moderating glycemic response relative to pure sugar solutions. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (55) × net available carbohydrate per serving (300 g × 7.86 g/100g = 23.58 g total carb; minus 0.99 g fibre = 21.1 g net carb) / 100 = 11.6. Source: University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care. Note: GI of 55 falls at the upper boundary of the low category (≤55) per the University of Sydney classification.

hypertension.key_factor: Added sugar content (6.57 g/100g) exceeds the 5 g/100g sugar-sweetened beverage threshold; sodium is low at 25 mg/100g
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this beverage due to its sugar content (6.57 g/100g), which independently elevates blood pressure risk via insulin-mediated sodium retention; request an unsweetened version or substitute with plain coconut water.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage with 6.57 g sugar/100g and low dietary fibre (0.33 g/100g), producing rapid postprandial glucose excursion
type2_diabetes.guidance: This beverage is unsuitable for Type 2 Diabetes management due to its high sugar load (6.57 g/100g) and negligible fibre (0.33 g/100g); liquid sugars are absorbed rapidly and cause significant postprandial glucose spikes — choose unsweetened coconut water or a non-nutritive sweetener alternative instead.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Estimated saturated fat approximately 2.80 g/100g from coconut ice cream base, combined with added sugar (6.57 g/100g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume this beverage only occasionally, as the combination of saturated fat from coconut ice cream and added sugar contributes to atherogenic lipid profiles and endothelial inflammation; an unsweetened, lower-fat coconut beverage is preferred.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Added sugar and coconut saturated fat contribute to pro-inflammatory load; no meaningful anti-inflammatory nutrients present
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: This beverage provides no anti-inflammatory benefit for rheumatoid arthritis — added sugar promotes inflammatory mediators and coconut saturated fat adds pro-inflammatory load; anti-inflammatory alternatives such as green tea or turmeric-ginger drinks are preferred.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Coconut Shake (No Topping) is a chilled blended beverage popular at hawker stalls and beverage outlets across Singapore and Malaysia. It is made by blending coconut ice cream with coconut water and fresh milk, producing a creamy, mildly sweet drink with a pronounced coconut flavour and smooth texture. No additional toppings such as nata de coco, grass jelly, or red bean are included in this variant. Per 100 g, the beverage provides 63 kcal, 0.54 g protein, 3.27 g fat (predominantly saturated from coconut), 7.86 g carbohydrate, and 6.57 g sugar, with low dietary fibre (0.33 g) and low sodium (25 mg). The high sugar-to-fibre ratio classifies this as a sugar-sweetened beverage, making it unsuitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease without modification. The glycemic index is estimated at 55 (low, at the upper boundary of the low GI category per University of Sydney classification), with a glycemic load of approximately 11.6 per 300 g serving. Clinically, the liquid sugar matrix results in rapid postprandial glucose absorption. The beverage is vegetarian, halal-friendly, and gluten-free, but contains dairy. It is commonly consumed as a snack or refreshment in tropical climates and is associated with Malaysian and Singaporean street food culture.

Coconut shake vs coconut water nutrition

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id D03060002) at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy coconut ice cream and coconut water composition as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this item. GI is an analogous estimate documented with approved source basis. All other fields are consistent with the HPB canonical record.
notes: Saturated fat (2.80 g/100g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy coconut ice cream and coconut water composition, as HPB Singapore Foodo does not separately report saturated fat for this item. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed micronutrient and fatty acid profile data for this specific coconut shake preparation are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. GI value of 55 is an analogous estimate based on blended dairy-coconut beverage with moderate sugar and fat content; no direct GI measurement for coconut shake was found in the University of Sydney GI Database as of 2026-04-16. GI of 55 is classified as 'low' per the University of Sydney standard (low ≤55, medium 56–69, high ≥70); this corrects the previous iteration's erroneous 'medium' classification. Food subgroup retained as 'Fruit smoothies' per HPB source classification, though this beverage is more accurately a coconut-based blended drink. Multilingual names provided for Singaporean/Malaysian cuisine context: Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Initial draft constructed from HPB source data.
Iteration 2: Corrected ontology match_type fields from 'approximate' to 'closest_parent'.

Iteration 3: Corrected gi_category from 'medium' to 'low' — GI of 55 falls at the upper boundary of the low category per University of Sydney standard classification (low ≤55, medium 56–69, high ≥70). Updated gi_source notes and llm_training description to reflect this correction. Updated notes field to document the correction.
